coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots derived from magnolia withered leaves for promoting growth and fluorescent labeling of bean sprouts
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Shaofeng
Wang, Muqun
Gao, Wei
Luo, Sa
Huang, Na
Qin, Yuxuan
coredata.dc:description: As a nano-fertilizer, carbon dots have good application prospects in crop planting. And its unique fluorescent properties and low toxicity make it become a good biomarker material. In this paper, CDs is prepared by a facile synthesis method. Mung bean as a model plant, and gradient concentration of N-CDs solution for breeding bean sprouts. The results show that 0.15 mg⋅L−1 carbon point solution can promote plant growth. At the same time, we also reveal that carbon dots segmented migration in bean sprouts, discuss the transfer path of carbon dots in bean sprouts, and find that the absorption of carbon dots solution by bean sprouts is linear, and its R2=0.98. We find that carbon dots can maintain good fluorescence properties in bean sprouts. A metabolic residue experiment is carried out and it is found that the carbon dots still exist in the bean sprouts after 24 h.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nano fertilizer
Biomarker

Dynamic absorption of CDs by bean sprouts
Metabolic residues of CDs in bean sprouts
Effect of CDs on bean sprouts and change of water content
Observation on the transport of CDs in bean sprouts

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bean sprouts were cultivated for 144 h in different concentrations of CDs. a length, b diameter, c water content.
(a) TEM (b) FTIR (c) Schematic diagram of CDs promoting water absorption of bean sprouts (d) Schematic diagram of CDs promoting bean sprout diameter growth.
(a) Number of split roots in 24 h. (b) Number of split roots in 48 h. (c) Number of split roots with 72 h. (d) XPS.
(a) Chlorophyll content change of bean sprouts (b) UV-vis absorbance(1) and fluorescence spectra of CDs(2-3)
(a) Net photosynthetic rate (b) Stomatal conductance (c) Transpiration rate (d) Intercellular CO2 concentration.
Migration of carbon dots in bean sprouts.
Root, stem and leaf sections of bean sprouts under UV irradiation.
(a) Dynamic absorption of CDs by bean sprouts (b) Linear fitting of CDs absorbed by bean sprouts.
(a) The stability of CDs (b)–(f) The stability of CDs in bean sprouts.
Metabolic residues of CDs in the roots and stems of bean sprouts.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots derived from magnolia withered leaves for promoting growth and fluorescent labeling of bean sprouts

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Guangxi, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Guangxi, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a nano-fertilizer, carbon dots have good application prospects in crop planting. And its unique fluorescent properties and low toxicity make it become a good biomarker material. In this paper, CDs is prepared by a facile synthesis method. Mung bean as a model plant, and gradient concentration of N-CDs solution for breeding bean sprouts. The results show that 0.15 mg⋅L−1 carbon point solution can promote plant growth. At the same time, we also reveal that carbon dots segmented migration in bean sprouts, discuss the transfer path of carbon dots in bean sprouts, and find that the absorption of carbon dots solution by bean sprouts is linear, and its R2=0.98. We find that carbon dots can maintain good fluorescence properties in bean sprouts. A metabolic residue experiment is carried out and it is found that the carbon dots still exist in the bean sprouts after 24 h.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) was first manufactured in 2004 [1]. This new member of the carbon nanometer material has advantages of tunable excitation and emission wavelengths, stable fluorescence, good biocompatibility and low biological toxicity, which has caused extensive concern [2]. Carbon dots has been used for a number of application, including electrodes for batteries [3,4], selective detection (metal ions [5–7],Tetracycline [8], Organic pollutants [9]), biological imaging [9,10],UV shielding [11]. Much work has been done on the optical properties of carbon dots, such as doping elements. N-CDs is studied most at present, and other elements doped CDs are discovered (such as P [12], S [13,14], Cl [15], B [16]). To enhance the photoluminescence of carbon dots, polyethylene glycol (PEG) as a passivator can enhance the PL [17]. Beside, Wei et al. [18] reported a method to enhance the fluorescence of CDs based on the increase of distance between functional carbon quantum dots.

As a nano-fertilizer, the application of carbon dots in agricultural planting has been gradually developed. In the past few years, Chakravarty et al. have used graphene carbon quantum dots to culture coriander and garlic plants, graphene carbon quantum dots can obviously improve the growth rate of plants [19]. Carbon dots have a dose effect on plant growth. At the appropriate concentration, carbon dots promote growth while promoting plant photosynthesis and increasing carbohydrate content in plants [20,21]. Zhang et al. [22] synthesized chiral CDs, both of L- and D-CDs could promote the growth of mung bean plants in root and stem length of mung bean sprouts and water absorption of bean seeds, and improve root vigor and the activity of Rubisco enzyme. A study also has shown that CDs can effectively increase the activity of ribose-1,5-diphosphate carboxylase (Rubisco) and then increase plant yield [23]. Due to the activity of Rubisco enzyme of mung bean sprouts enhanced, which accumulate more carbohydrate of mung bean plants and grow better.

Compared with traditional metallic carbon quantum dots, CDs have low toxicity and environmental friendliness. CDs are an ideal material for biomarking. Loukanov et al. [24] investigated the real time monitoring and quantification of the concentration of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (C-dots) in eukaryotic Tobacco bright yellow-2 (BY-2) plant cells, the cells were in good health conditions and any blinking artifacts were not observed. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been widely studied in fluorescent labeling. Li et al. [25] made a study that mung bean was adopted as a model plant to study the phytotoxicity, uptake, and translocation of red emissive CDs in plants. CDs imposed no phytotoxicity on mung bean growth, which were transferred from the roots to the stems and leaves by the vascular system through the apoplastic pathway.

Magnolia trees are widely distributed in southern China, which can grow at temperatures above −20–40 °C, suitable for campus greening. As experimental materials, it was abundant. In this work, using the withered leaves of Magnolia as the precursor, a green synthesis method was used to synthesize CDs, XPS showed that the carbon dots contained N elements (2.65%). The N-CDs (0.15 mg⋅mL−1) solution was found to show the best growth rate for growing bean sprouts. After 144 h of cultivation, the length of bean sprouts cultured in N-CDs solution was obviously better than that in pure water. CDs also provides biomarkers while promoting growth, the blue fluorescence was observed under 365 nm UV irradiation, and the blue fluorescence migrated over time to the leaf position of bean sprouts. The CDs entering the bean sprouts still maintain good optical properties and can exist in the bean sprouts for a long time.
Withered leaves of the magnolia trees were collected from Guangxi University, Guangxi, China. And all reagents were of analytical grade and were purchased from Aladdin.

The CDs were prepared based on a reported method [5]. The method of CDs preparation was as follows:(1) Cleaned withered leaves with water, which were heated at 60 °C for 12 h in the air to ensure leaves are completely dried; (2) Weight 4 g fully dried withered leaves, chopped, and placed in a muffle furnace, 350 °C, for 30 min; (3) The black powder was added 200 mL purified water and 5 mLH2O2, stirred at room temperature for 12 h; (4) Using a 0.22 micron filter membrane to remove impurities to obtain a yellowish-brown liquid;(5) Dialysis bag (MD:100) was used to purify the target product for 24 h;(6) Freeze-dried into powder for later testing.

The morphology analysis uses FEI TECNAI G2 F30 300kv field emission transmission electron microscope; the element analysis uses the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy produced by Thermo Fisher Scientific (model: ESCALAB 250XI+); the functional group test uses the Fourier produced by Thermo Fisher Scientific Infrared spectrometer (model: Nicolet iS50); highly sensitive and stable transient fluorescence spectrometer (model: FL3C-111 TCSPC) produced by Japan HORIBA for the measurement of fluorescence emission intensity.

Measure the integrated fluorescence intensity of the same concentration (0.05mol⋅L−1) of quinine sulfate solution and CDs at the same excitation wavelength, and measure the absorbance of the excitation light at that wavelength at the same time, and calculate the CDs fluorescence quantum yield according to the following formula [26]:
(1)
Y U
=
Y S
×
F U
F S
×
A S
A U

Y U
and
Y S
, respectively represent the fluorescence quantum yields of the test substance and quinine sulfate;
F U
and
F S
, respectively represent the integrated fluorescence intensity of the test substance and the reference substance;
A U
and
A S
, respectively represent the absorbance of the test substance and the reference substance.

First, configure the CDs powder into 6 gradient concentration solutions (0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25 mol⋅L−1). Then, each concentration of CDs solution (5 mL) and Oocystis (20 pieces) were placed in a petri dish and incubated for 24 h at 28 °C. Five samples for each group.

A certain amount of mung beans was soaked in pure water for 12 h. Self-made simple bean sprouts cultivation box, seeds of mung beans of similar size were soaked in pure water and different concentrations of CD solution (0.05.0.1.0.15,0.2,0.25 mg⋅mL−1) and grown at room temperature without light for 3 days. The control group is pure water, the experimental group is a CD solution. After 3 days, the culture box containing mung beans was placed outside for 3 days. About 6 days, harvest the mung bean sprouts.

Randomly selected 15 bean sprouts to measure the length and diameter of the culture box with different concentrations of CDs of 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25 mg/ml. The measurement interval was 24 h,48 h,72 h, 96 h, 120 h, 144 h, used a ruler to measure the length of bean sprouts and an electronic vernier caliper to the diameter of bean sprouts. After 144 h, weighted 15 bean sprouts, placed 60 °C constant temperature drying box for 48 h, took out and weighted again.

Determine the chlorophyll content with the investigated method [27]. Collect 0.2 g of fresh leaves, rinse with distilled water, and place in test tubes. Add 10 ml acetone/ethanol extract (1: 1, v / v) to the tube, shake for 14 h in the dark, then extract the chlorophyll and use the same extract to dilute the solution to 25 ml. The absorbance of the supernatant was measured at wavelengths of 645 and 663 nm. The chlorophyll content was calculated using the following formulas (2–4):
(2)
C h l o r o p h y l l
a
c o n c e n t r a t i o n
(
g ·
L
− 1
)
= 0.0127
A 663
− 0.00269
A 645
(3)
C h l o r o p h y l l
b
c o n c e n t r a t i o n
(
g ·
L
− 1
)
= 0.0229
A 645
− 0.00468
A 663
(4)
T o t a l
c h l o r o p h y l l
c o n c e n t r a t i o n = C h l
a + C h l
b
The activity of the mung bean sprout photosystem was performed by the LI-6400XT photosynthesis analyzer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Macro transport of CDs in bean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: The bean sprouts had been germinated for 3 days at room temperature in dark were transferred into a beaker containing 100 ml 0.15 mg/mL CDs. Took out a bean sprout every 6 h and observed and photographed with UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Distribution of CDs in bean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: On based on 2.10, the bean sprouts were soaked in CDs solution for 2 days. The roots, stems, cotyledons, and leaves of mung bean were sliced, the distribution of CDs in plants was observed by microscope. Before observation, the bean sprouts were thoroughly cleaned with deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic absorption of CDs by bean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the adsorption process of carbon point solution by plants. The whole process of the plant absorbing CDs, CDs solution was collected every 12 h, the fluorescence spectrum is used to test the change of fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metabolic residues of CDs in bean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: Ensuring the CDs had spread to the whole body of bean sprouts, the bean sprouts were transferred from the CDs to pure water for fostering, and the bean sprouts were taken out every 12 h to observe and investigate the metabolic residues of CDs in the bean sprouts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on bean sprouts and change of water content
ce:section.ce:para: Before carrying out biological cultivation and biomarker experiments, we verified the toxicity of CDs (Fig. S1). Configure the obtained powder (product yield: 5.75%) into solutions of different concentrations. We selected Oocystis as the experimental object and observed its survival number under different concentrations. Under the high concentration of 0.25 mg⋅mL−1 CDs, the survival rate of Oocystis is still greater than 80%, and the survival rate of Oocystis is close to or even greater than 90% at the low concentration of CDs.

As shown in Fig. 1 , Fig. 1a shows that bean sprouts were cultured in a gradient concentration of the CDs. After 144 h of incubation, the group of 0.15 mg⋅mL−1 CDs showed the best growth. After 6 days of cultivation, the average growth of bean sprouts cultivated with 0.15 mg⋅mL−1 CDs reached a maximum of 30.11 cm. In Fig. 1b, it can be seen that during the growth of bean sprouts, along with the change in diameter. As the concentration of the CDs increases, the peak value of the diameter change does not appear at 0.15 mg⋅mL−1 but appears as 0.2 mg⋅mL−1. The corresponding peak average diameter is 2.61 mm. Fig. 1c shows the change in water content of bean sprouts, and it can be seen that the maximum water content appears at 0.15 mg⋅mL−1. Fig. 2 b is the infrared spectrum of CDs. At 3678 cm−1 there is a broad absorption belonging to the stretching vibration of the -COOH bonds, while the strong absorption at 3170 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H. The absorption at 1718 cm−1 belongs to the vibration mode of C = O, and the asymmetric stretching vibration of the =C-H bond will cause an absorption peak at 2160 cm−1 [28]. The absorption at 1398 cm−1 and 1033 cm−1 belong to COO− bond and C-O-C bond, respectively [29]. FTIR shows that CDs carry active bonds, which can transport more water. Fig. 2a has shown that the particle size of the CDs is at the nanometer level, which provides the basis for entering the bean sprouts, so as shown in the Fig. 2c, the CDs can carry more water into the bean sprouts and directly increase the water content of the bean sprouts. The maximum diameter of bean sprouts appears at 0.2 mg⋅mL−1, as shown in Fig. 2d, compared with 0.15 mg⋅mL−1 CDs solution, 0.2 mg⋅mL−1 CDs has shown growth inhibitory effect, and the length of bean sprouts cannot be increased, and under the dual conditions that CDs carry a large amount of water into the bean sprouts that will grow horizontally, showing the result of an increase in diameter.

In the process of cultivating bean sprouts, we observed an increase in the number of split roots in bean sprouts cultivated with CDs (Fig. 3 a–c). This is a point that has been neglected in many existing works that CDs to promote plant growth. We performed XPS analysis on the CDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 3d. The measured spectrum presents three representative peaks: C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV), and O1s (532 eV). Deconvolute the spectrum (Fig. S2), C1s:C = C/C-C (284 eV), C-O(286 eV); N1s:C-N-C(398 eV), C-N-H(399 eV), (C)3-N(400 eV); O1s:C = O(528 eV), C-O-C(530 eV), C-OH (531 eV). CDs contain nitrogen related groups, and the plant growth process cannot do without the three elements of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium. We use the withered leaves of the white magnolia tree as the raw material to prepare the CDs. The nitrogen element contained in the raw material itself exists in the form of groups on the CDs. The CDs carrying the N element are equivalent to the nitrogen fertilizers used in our lives. Curve fitting is performed on the average number of divided roots of bean sprouts cultivated by different concentrations of carbon points. The distribution of divided roots of bean sprouts at 24 h of exposure conforms to the Gauss Model, with R2 = 0.9815, and the distribution of divided roots of bean sprouts at 48 and 72 h of exposure conforms to the Gauss Model. In Gauss Amp Model, the R2 values are 0.9841 and 0.9786, respectively. It can be found that the fitting degrees of the distribution curves of the number of divided roots in the three different time periods are all close to 1.

Chlorophyll is an important pigment in the photosynthesis process, used to convert light energy into chemical energy. The increase in the chlorophyll content of the plant is beneficial for the efficiency of light absorption and for the formation of carbohydrates in photosynthesis and is more conducive to the future growth of plants. As shown in Fig. 4 a, compared with the blank group, the maximum chlorophyll content appeared at 0.15 mg/mL after 72 incubation, the chlorophyll content increased by 36.19%, the CDs may increase the chlorophyll content.

Fig. 4b shows the UV-vis absorbance and fluorescence spectrum of carbon dots. The CDs emits blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light, and the quantum yield is 13.20% (Table.S1). It can be seen from the ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum that there are π-π* transitions and n-π* transitions in the CDs, and the optimal excitation wavelength of CDs is 362 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength is 433 nm. We excited the CDs with wavelengths separated by 10 nm, and there was a red shift in the emission peak position (Fig.S3). CDs have strong absorption in the ultraviolet region, while chloroplasts do not. In addition, CD exhibits strong blue light, which covers the absorption spectrum of chloroplasts well, indicating that CD has the potential to improve plant photosynthetic efficiency [23].

For the test of photosystem activity, we evaluate it from four indicators: net photosynthetic rate, stomatal conductance, transpiration rate, and intercellular carbon dioxide concentration. From Fig. 5 b and c, when the concentration of carbon dots is 0.15 mg⋅mL−1, the stomatal conductance and transpiration rate reach the peak. While the bio-based nano carbon dots increase the stomatal conductance, it also accelerates the transpiration rate of bean sprout leaves, which is beneficial to improving the absorption and transportation of water by mung bean seedlings is conducive to the growth of mung bean seedlings [30]. This is consistent with the result that the water content of bean sprouts cultivated with 0.15 mg⋅mL−1 carbon dot solution is the highest. In Fig. 5a, the trend of the net photosynthetic rate is consistent with the trend of the chlorophyll content of bean sprouts, and the chlorophyll content has a direct effect on the net photosynthetic rate. The high stomatal conductance is more conducive to the progress of photosynthesis, so the bean sprouts cultivated with 0.15 mg⋅mL−1 carbon dots have the strongest photosynthesis in the experimental group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation on the transport of CDs in bean sprouts
ce:section.ce:para: During the cultivation process, we observed that there is a migration phenomenon of CDs in the bean sprouts. After 6 h of cultivation in a 0.15 mg⋅mL−1 CDs solution, which entered the roots of the bean sprouts and emitted obvious blue light under UV light. After the bean sprouts absorbed the CDs for 12 h, the CDs were almost distributed throughout the root. Over time, the CDs have migrated to the stems of the bean sprouts and finally reached the leaves. As the position of the bean sprouts moves up, the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots decreases visible. In the process of observation, we found that there is a bright fluorescence between the stem and root of the bean sprouts during the process of absorbing carbon dots. In the process of segment migration, carbon dots are first enriched at the end of the root and then begin to transfer, and then enriched at the end of the stem and migrate to the leaf part (Fig. 6 ).

To explore the absorption of carbon dots by bean sprouts in-depth, we sliced the bean sprouts and observed them with ultraviolet light (Fig. 7 ). When we sliced the roots of bean sprouts, we can see the root ducts. Under UV irradiation, the position of the root ducts of the bean sprouts emits conspicuous blue fluorescence. Then observe the stems of the bean sprouts. Under the 400 times magnification of the optical microscope, the tube (spiral) of the stem of the bean sprouts was photographed. Under the excitation of 365 nm ultraviolet light, the tube of the stem emits blue fluorescence, and finally, we can see the veins of leaves also show blue fluorescence.

Therefore, we can speculate on the mechanism of CDs migration in bean sprouts. First of all, the CDs have a nanoscale particle size and good water solubility, which provides the basis for the CDs to enter the body of the bean sprouts. When the roots of the bean sprouts absorb water, the CDs will enter the body along with the water, which enters the bean sprouts through the tube part of the bean sprouts and migrates in the bean sprouts with the flow of water, and finally transfer to the leaf position of the bean sprouts through the vein.

There is a dynamic absorption of CDs in bean sprouts. It can be seen from Fig. 8 a that the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases gradually every 12 h. From 0 to 36 h, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreases the most, that is the reason why the root of bean sprouts is highly fluorescent because bean sprouts absorb a large number of CDs and hoard in the root position. Fitting the fluorescence data of the carbon point of bean sprouts (Fig. 8b), it was found that the process of absorbing carbon points of bean sprouts showed a linear correlation, R2 = 0.98.

At the same time, we also discussed the stability of CDs in bean sprouts. First, we verified the stability of the CDs themselves, exposed the CDs to the air, and measured the fluorescence intensity of the CDs solution at 24 h intervals. It can be seen from Fig. 9 a that after exposing the CDs to the air, after five days, the fluorescence value of the CDs themselves did not change significantly. We can conclude that CDs have good stability. Fig. 9(b)–(f), we investigated the stability of the CDs entering the plant body and found that the CDs can still maintain good stability in the plant and have good fluorescence properties. This discovery plays an important role in the application of CDs in plant biomarkers.

Finally, we analyzed the metabolic residues of carbon dots in bean sprouts. Fig. 10 (a) and (d) show that the CDs have been distributed in the roots and stems of the bean sprouts. We put the bean sprouts with CDs back into pure water, we found that the fluorescence of the roots was significantly weakened after 12 h, but the fluorescence effect of the stem is still obvious. After 24 h, we no longer observe blue fluorescence in the roots of the bean sprouts, but there is still a fluorescence effect in the stems. We can guess that the CDs are metabolized faster at the roots of the bean sprouts. As water continues to enter the bean sprouts, the CDs at the roots will be first metabolized by the bean sprouts. After 24 h, the CDs still exist in the stems of the bean sprouts. On the one hand, it shows that the metabolism of CDs in the stems of bean sprouts is slower than that of the roots, on the other hand, it reflects that CDs have better biocompatibility and can exist in the bean sprouts for a longer time.

In this paper, the withered leaves of the Magnolia were used as precursors to prepare nano-fluorescent carbon dots for cultivating bean sprouts. Under the cultivation of the 0.15 mg⋅mL−1 CDs, the growth of bean sprouts reaches the best and the root growth of bean sprouts is promoted. At the same time, it was found that under 0.2 mg⋅mL−1 CDs, the diameter growth of bean sprouts reached an average value of 2.48 mm, which was an increase of 12.07% compared to the control. We also revealed that the absorption of CDs by bean sprouts is a stepwise migration and found that the CDs are transported in the bean sprout body through a tube, and we concluded that the absorption of CDs by bean sprouts is linear. We also found that the carbon dots can stably maintain their optical properties in the bean sprouts. The good biocompatibility made the carbon dots will not be easily metabolized by the bean sprouts. This research completed the work of CDs to promote the growth of bean sprouts and the integration of visualized transportation in the bean sprouts, which has a certain guiding role in the cultivation of crops.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cartre.2021.100063.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments

Baker, S.N.; Baker, G.A., Luminescent carbon nanodots: emergent nanolights. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.2010, 49 (38), 6726–6744.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent carbon dots derived from Mangifera indica leaves for selective detection of metal ions

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from gardenia fruit for sensitive on-off-on detection of Hg2+ and cysteine

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of homogeneous carbon quantum dots by ultrafast dual-beam pulsed laser ablation for bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cui, L.; Ren, X.; Wang, J.; Sun, M., Synthesis of homogeneous carbon quantum dots by ultrafast dual-beam pulsed laser ablation for bioimaging. Mater. Today Nano, 12 (2020) 100091.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual roles of a transparent polymer film containing dispersed N-doped carbon dots: a high-efficiency blue light converter and UV screen

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Barman, B.K.; Nagao, T.; Nanda, K.K., Dual roles of a transparent polymer film containing dispersed N-doped carbon dots: a high-efficiency blue light converter and UV screen. Appl. Surf. Sci., 510 (2020) 145405.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent N,S,P tri-doped carbon dots for Cl- detection and their assistance of TiO2 as the catalyst in the degradation of methylene blue

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The N,S co-doped carbon dots with excellent luminescent properties from green tea leaf residue and its sensing of gefitinib
